commands.review_summary:
The PR removes write-time media pruning from `saveMediaSource`, narrows `cleanOldMedia` to the gateway timer path, updates timer tests, and clarifies `media.ttlHours` help text.

PR surface: Source -6, Tests -46. Total -52 across 5 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible: current main saves media through `saveMediaSource`, immediately calls `cleanOldMedia(DEFAULT_TTL_MS, { recursive: false })`, and the inspected fs-safe dependency descends one level when `maxDepth: 1` is set. I did not execute a live repro in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] No automated code repair is needed; the remaining blockers are contributor proof and maintainer acceptance/documentation of the cleanup default change.

target.title: fix(media): stop pruning media on write; let the configured timer do it
target.body:
## Summary

`saveMediaSource` ran an inline `cleanOldMedia(DEFAULT_TTL_MS, { recursive: false })`
on every call. Two problems compounded:

1. `DEFAULT_TTL_MS` is **2 minutes**, hardcoded — it ignores the configurable
   `media.ttlHours` retention.
2. `cleanOldMedia` mapped `{ recursive: false }` to `pruneExpired({ maxDepth: 1, … })`.
   The `@openclaw/fs-safe` prune walker keys descent off `maxDepth` whenever it is set
   (`shouldDescend = maxDepth !== undefined ? depth < maxDepth : recursive`), so
   `recursive: false` still descended one level — into `media/inbound/`,
   `media/outbound/`, `media/tool-image-generation/`, etc.

Net effect: preparing _any_ outgoing attachment silently mass-deleted unrelated
attachments and AI-generated images older than 2 minutes across all first-level
`media/` subdirectories, regardless of `media.ttlHours`. `media/` is canonical storage
now (not the temporary Twilio scratch dir it was when this was added in `948ff7f`), so
this is destructive legacy behavior. `pruneExpired` swallows per-file errors, so the
deletions were unlogged.

## Fix

Remove the inline cleanup from `saveMediaSource`. Media cleanup is now owned solely by
the gateway maintenance timer (`startGatewayMaintenanceTimers`), which runs a full-tree
recursive sweep using the operator-configured `media.ttlHours`. With `media.ttlHours`
unset (default), no automatic cleanup runs — cleanup is opt-in.

Because the timer is the only caller and always uses `{ recursive: true,
pruneEmptyDirs: true }`, the now-dead shallow / `recursive: false` modes, the
`CleanOldMediaOptions` type, and the `DEFAULT_TTL_MS` constant are removed.
`cleanOldMedia(ttlMs)` now always does the full-tree recursive sweep.

## Behavior change

- Writing media (`saveMediaSource` / `saveMediaBuffer` / `saveMediaStream`) never prunes.
- Media is only pruned when `media.ttlHours` is set, via the maintenance timer.
- `media.ttlHours` unset → no automatic media cleanup (help text updated accordingly).

## Verification

- The `@openclaw/fs-safe@0.3.0` `pruneExpiredStoreEntries` descent logic was ported into
  a standalone Node reproduction and run against real temp directories, confirming the
  old `{ recursive: false }` deleted `media/inbound/<file>` (the reported bug) and that
  `cleanOldMedia(ttlMs)` removes root-level and first-level expired files and does not
  follow symlinked directories.
- The gateway timer's effective `pruneExpired` arguments are unchanged
  (`recursive: true, pruneEmptyDirs: true`, `maxDepth` undefined), so the recursive
  cleanup tests retain their original behavior.
- Ran the related test suites locally — `pnpm test src/media/store.test.ts
  src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts` — and all tests pass. Type-check
  (`pnpm tsgo`) is clean.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)
No

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)
No

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)
No

What is the highest-risk area?
Media file cleanup

How is that risk mitigated?
Ran code to confirm file cleanup works as expected.

## Current review state

What is the next action?


What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?


Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

<details>
<summary>Review state guidance</summary>

Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

</details>
